description: When Akrura drove his chariot to Vrindavan to fetch Krishna, the words that rose in him became this stuti, preserved in the Shrimad Bhagavata Purana 10.40. Its eleven verses are a charioteer's prayer to Vasudeva, the supreme one who lies hidden behind maya. Devotees turn to it to deepen their love for Krishna, to grasp the philosophy of the supreme, to shed the ignorance that maya breeds, and to draw down the Lord's grace and compassion. Wednesday and Thursday are the favoured days, and the recitation gains weight during Janmashtami and on Ekadashi. Reading the full set unhurried runs about eight minutes. The page carries the complete text in Devanagari, an English transliteration alongside it, the Hindi arth, and a PDF you can download and keep for offline chanting.

Eight forms of Lakshmi, one hymn. The Ashtalakshmi Stotram, carried down through tradition, gives a verse to each face of the goddess so that no door of fortune stays shut. It calls on Adi Lakshmi, the primordial wealth, and Dhanya Lakshmi of the food grains; on Dhairya Lakshmi for courage and Gaja Lakshmi for royal power; on Santana Lakshmi for progeny and Vijaya Lakshmi for victory. Recited together, the eight stanzas ask for prosperity that is whole rather than partial, and for steady spiritual growth alongside it. Friday is the day devotees keep for it, and it rings loudest during Diwali and through Navratri. The reading takes roughly ten minutes. Here you will find all eight verses in Devanagari, a clear transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline use.

Picture the infant Krishna lying on a banyan leaf, toe in mouth, and you have the image this hymn holds in front of the heart. The Bala Mukundashtakam, an eight-verse piece from the Vaishnavite stotra tradition, is among the best-loved of all Krishna prayers. Meditating on that baby form is said to wash the heart clean, to bring the blessing of children and their protection, and to fill the chanter with a joy, peace, and divine love that needs no occasion. Wednesdays suit it, and it grows in tenderness during Janmashtami and Govardhan Puja. Five minutes covers the eight verses. The full text sits here in Devanagari with a side-by-side transliteration, the Hindi arth for each line, and a PDF you can download to recite away from the screen.

A kavacham is armour, and this one is forged for the body part by part. Drawn from the Rudrayamala Tantra, the Bhairav Kavacham wraps the devotee in Bhairav's protection from head to toe across its nine verses. Tradition invokes it to raise a divine shield through Bhairav's grace, to guard against evil spirits, black magic, and occult attack, and to dissolve fear in the places where fear is sharpest, battlefields and lonely roads among them. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, and it carries special force on Kalashtami and at Maha Shivaratri. Allow around ten minutes for the full recitation. The page presents every verse in Devanagari with a matching English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a downloadable PDF so the armour can be chanted anywhere.

Few offerings please Shiva like a single bilva leaf, and this hymn from the Shiva Rahasya tradition spends twelve verses explaining why. The Bilvarchana Stotram glorifies the supreme merit of that humble leaf laid at Shiva's feet, declaring that one bilva offering equals crores of Kanyadaan and yields the fruit of a pilgrimage to Kashi and Prayag. Recited with faith, it is held to burn away the sins of three lifetimes. Monday is the day kept for it, and it deepens during Maha Shivaratri and on the Shravan Mondays. The twelve verses take about seven minutes to read. On this page the complete text appears in Devanagari, paired with an English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF you can keep for offline recitation.

Among the Navagraha hymns, this short prayer is the one addressed to the moon. Coming down from the Purana tradition, the Chandra Stotram packs its work into five verses: it salutes Chandra in all his forms, asks for planetary peace and divine protection, and is recited to lift afflictions and restore auspiciousness, all while feeding the devotee's spiritual growth. Monday, the moon's own day, is when it belongs, and Sharad Purnima, the night the moon stands fullest, is its festival. Three minutes is enough to chant the whole thing, which makes it easy to fold into a morning or evening routine. You will find the five verses here in Devanagari with an English transliteration running beside them, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF available for free download.

Bavani means fifty-two, and that is exactly how many verses this Gujarati-rooted hymn to Dattatreya carries. Belonging to the traditional Dattatreya devotional literature, the Datta Bavani is reached for in times of trouble: the old tradition invokes it to clear away calamities, dangers, and sorrows, and it is said that reading it in the morning lifts all suffering. Its verses carry the wisdom of Dattatreya's twenty-four gurus and the combined blessings of Brahma, Vishnu, and Mahesha gathered in one form. Thursday is the day for it, with Guru Purnima its high point. The fifty-two verses run to about ten minutes. The page gives the full Devanagari text, a readable English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF for those who prefer to chant offline.

Hridayam means heart, and this is the heart-essence of the Divine Mother herself, a supreme Shakti text from the Atharvana tradition handed down through Bhargava Rishi. Across its eight verses the Devi Hridayam embraces every form of the goddess, Mahalakshmi, Mahakali, and Mahasaraswati together, and it carries the Anganyasa with the Shakti bija mantras Hreem, Shreem, Aim, and Kleem. Devotees take it up for sarva siddhi and for union with the Mother. Friday and Tuesday are its days; during Navratri and Durga Puja it comes fully alive. The recitation lasts roughly seven minutes. Everything is laid out on this page: the complete verses in Devanagari, an English transliteration set against them, the Hindi meaning line by line, and a PDF you can download without charge.

A small boy stood firm in penance until Vishnu appeared and touched his cheek with a conch, and the praise that poured out of Dhruva is recorded in the Shrimad Bhagavata Purana, Canto 4, Chapter 9. Its nine verses model the kind of unwavering resolve and devotion that the child himself showed. Reciting them is said to win the company of pure devotees, the sat-sanga, and to help one cross the ocean of material existence, while teaching the plain truth that God alone is the true refuge, not the desires we chase. Thursday and Sunday are its days, and Ekadashi is its occasion. About eight minutes covers the nine verses. The page holds the full Dhruva Stuti in Devanagari, with English transliteration, Hindi arth, and a free downloadable PDF.

Five jewels, five verses. The Durga Pancha Ratnam, drawn from the Devi Mahatmya tradition, is deliberately short and deliberately dense, a compact prayer that still carries the full weight of the Mother's protection. Each of its five stanzas asks for shelter from danger and calamity, and the hymn names Durga as Narayani, the supreme feminine power behind all creation. Tuesday and Friday are the days devotees set aside for it, and during Navratri and Durga Puja it is recited with particular fervour. Because it runs only about three minutes, it fits a busy morning as easily as a long temple sitting. On this page you can read all five verses in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration, study the Hindi meaning, and download a PDF for offline chanting.

Why fast on Ekadashi? These eight shlokas, gathered from the Padma Purana and Vishnu Purana, answer that question directly. They set out the supreme importance of the Ekadashi fast, declaring that it surpasses even the Ashvamedha yajna and a bath in the Ganga in the merit it earns, and that on this day all sins take refuge in food, which is why fasting becomes binding. The verses promise dharma, artha, kama, and moksha through a single day's discipline, which is why they count as essential reading for Vaishnava devotees and anyone who keeps the Ekadashi vow. They are recited on Vaikuntha Ekadashi, Nirjala Ekadashi, and Devuthani Ekadashi. The reading takes about three minutes. The page offers the Devanagari text, English transliteration, Hindi arth, and a free PDF.

Adi Shankaracharya set five jewel-verses to a galloping metre, and the result is the most popular Ganesha stotra in the whole Sanskrit tradition. The Ganesha Pancharatnam moves quickly because Shankaracharya wanted it chanted, not laboured over. In its five stanzas the tradition invokes the elephant-faced lord to clear obstacles and grant success, and to bestow wisdom, prosperity, and steady spiritual growth. Wednesday is Ganesha's day and the natural one for this hymn, while Ganesh Chaturthi is its festival. The reading lasts roughly four minutes, short enough that many learn it by heart. Here the complete text waits in Devanagari, with an English transliteration beside it, the Hindi meaning for each verse, and a PDF you can download free for offline recitation.

One verse from the Rigveda, hymn 3.62.10, and it is called the Mother of all Vedas, Veda Mata. The Gayatri Mantra is a single line of Vedic meditation that asks the divine light to awaken the intellect. Chanted at the Sandhya hours, it is held to bring immense spiritual merit, to sharpen the mind and grant wisdom and discrimination, to purify body, mind, and soul, and to dissolve ignorance on the way to enlightenment. Sunday is the day traditionally kept for it. Though it is a single verse, sitting with it for about three minutes settles the practice into a daily rhythm. On this page you can read the Gayatri Mantra in Devanagari with an English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF to download and carry.

This hymn keeps its gaze on the child Krishna, Bala-Gopala, and follows him through every lila of his early years, from the night of his birth to his days in Vrindavan. Handed down through tradition, the Gopala Stotram runs to twelve verses of that childhood play and innocence. Parents recite it for progeny, for the welfare and protection of children, and for wealth in cattle, the go-sampatti, with the agricultural abundance that follows. Wednesday is its day, and Janmashtami and Gopashtami its festivals. The twelve verses take around eight minutes to read aloud. The page sets out the complete text in Devanagari, with a side-by-side English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF you can download at no cost for offline chanting.

This is not a single chant but a small collection, gathering the Mula Mantra, the Gayatri, and the Namaskara verses of Dattatreya into nine units, with the famous "Digambara Digambara Shripada Vallabha" refrain among them. Sourced from the Guru Charitra and the Dattatreya Tantra, the Guru Dattatreya Mantra invokes the Trimurti through Dattatreya's single form, and is recited to break the cycle of birth and death and grant liberation. Thursday, the guru's day, is when it belongs, and Datta Jayanti and Guru Purnima are its festivals. The nine verses take about five minutes. On this page the full set appears in Devanagari, with English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Mangala means auspicious, and an ashtakam is eight verses, so this is eight stanzas of blessing called down upon and through Hanuman. Belonging to the Hanuman tradition, the Hanuman Mangalashtakam invokes the lord's auspicious power, banishes fear of every kind while granting courage, and is said to bestow the ashta siddhi and nava nidhi through Hanuman's grace, with strong protection from evil spirits and negative energies. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, and Hanuman Jayanti its festival. Five minutes covers the eight verses, light enough for daily chanting. The complete text is here in Devanagari, set alongside an English transliteration, with the Hindi meaning for each verse and a PDF you can download free to keep for offline use.

Twelve names of Indra, king of the devas, drawn straight from the Rig Veda, gathered into five short verses. The Indra Dwadash Nama Stotram is a name-litany rather than a long narrative hymn, and reciting it is held to call down every form of wealth and prosperity, to invoke timely rainfall and the agricultural abundance that depends on it, and to bestow aisvarya, lordship, and power by Indra's grace. Thursday and Sunday are the days kept for it. The five verses take only about three minutes, which suits a quick daily devotion. On this page you can read the full stotram in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration set beside it, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF for offline recitation.

Adi Shankaracharya is said to have composed these eight verses gazing at the great wooden form of Jagannath at Puri, and the refrain returns again and again to that single longing: may that Jagannath be the object of my sight. The Jagannatha Ashtakam invokes the lord's divine darshan, purifies the soul and destroys sins, leads the devotee to Vishnu-loka after death, and loosens attachment to worldly desires. Janmashtami is the festival on which it is most often recited. The eight verses run to about eight minutes. The page presents the complete text in Devanagari with an English transliteration beside it, the Hindi arth line by line, and a PDF available for free download so the hymn can be chanted offline.

Fourteen verses out of the Tantra Shastra, addressed to Mahakali in her fiercest aspect. The Kali Stotram does not soften the goddess; it calls on her protective ferocity to scatter enemies, evil forces, and negative energies, and to cut through deep-rooted fear until fearlessness remains. Recited three times a day, it is said to settle a stable prosperity, the sthira Lakshmi that does not slip away. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, and it carries weight through Navratri, on Kali Puja, and at Diwali. The fourteen verses take roughly eight minutes to read. On this page the full text is given in Devanagari, with an English transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning for every verse, and a free downloadable PDF for offline chanting.

From the Skanda Purana comes this eight-verse benediction to Kartikeya, the warrior son of Shiva who leads the armies of the gods. The Kartikeya Mangalashtakam calls down his auspicious blessings, asks him to rout enemies and negative forces, and seeks the courage, valour, and victory that mark his nature, along with freedom from disease and the gift of health. Tuesday is the day held for it, and Skanda Shashti and Kartikeya Jayanti are its festivals. The eight verses take about five minutes, easy to keep up as a daily practice. The page lays out the complete hymn in Devanagari, with a clear English transliteration beside it, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a PDF you can download free for offline recitation.

Two Gayatri variations live inside this short collection, one calling Krishna as Devakinandana, the son of Devaki, the other as Govinda. Drawn from the Bhagavata Purana and the Garga Samhita, the Krishna Gayatri Mantra opens with a dhyanam that paints the complete form of Krishna before the meditation begins. Its four verses are reached for to kindle intense Krishna bhakti and divine love, and they are said to lead toward Goloka, Krishna's supreme abode. Wednesday is its day, with particular power on Janmashtami, on Wednesdays, and at Ekadashi, and it also belongs to Holi. The four verses take about four minutes. Here the full text appears in Devanagari with English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline use.

Kubera keeps the treasury of the gods, and these five jewel-verses from the Vishnu Purana are addressed to him. Short and to the point, the Kubera Pancharatnam salutes the lord of wealth in all his forms, asks for divine protection and auspiciousness, and is recited to lift sins and bestow spiritual merit alongside material grace. Friday and Thursday are the days kept for it, and Diwali and Dhanteras, when households invite wealth across the threshold, are its festivals. At just two minutes the recitation is among the briefest, which makes it simple to add to any daily round. On this page you can read all five verses in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration, study the Hindi arth, and download a free PDF for offline recitation.

Each verse of this hymn ends with the same plea, karavalambam, give me your hand to hold, which is why it is also known as the Karavalamba Stotram. Composed by Adi Shankaracharya, the Lakshmi Narasimha Stotram runs to eight verses that compare worldly fears to fire, snake, and elephant and beg Narasimha to pull the devotee clear of all three. Because it joins Lakshmi's wealth to Narasimha's protection, it asks for both at once, and tradition holds it extremely powerful for liberation. Saturday is its day, and Narasimha Jayanti its festival. The eight verses take about ten minutes to read with care. The page carries the complete text in Devanagari, an English transliteration beside it, the Hindi meaning, and a free downloadable PDF.

An impenetrable armour of Mahakali, assembled verse by verse. Taken from the Kalika Purana, the Mahakali Kavacham assigns a different form of Devi to guard each part of the body and sets watch over all eight directions against negative energies. Across its eight verses it is recited to lift fear, disease, and every calamity, and devotees take it up for the kind of total protection a kavacham is built to give. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, with Kali Puja and Navratri its festivals. The reading runs about eight minutes. On this page the full text is presented in Devanagari, with an English transliteration set against it, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a PDF you can download free so the armour can be chanted offline.

Maruti is one of Hanuman's oldest names, son of the wind, and these eight verses praise him in his full power. Coming down through tradition, the Maruti Ashtakam is recited to drive enemies and obstacles out of one's life, to draw on courage, strength, and fearlessness, and to see desires fulfilled through Hanuman's grace, for the hymn likens him to a kalpa-vriksha that shelters those who turn to it. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, and Hanuman Jayanti its festival. The eight verses take roughly seven minutes to read aloud. Here you will find the complete text in Devanagari, an English transliteration running alongside it, the Hindi meaning line by line, and a PDF available for free download for offline recitation.

This is the Upanishad that gave India its national motto, Satyameva Jayate, truth alone triumphs, and these eleven key verses from the Mundaka Upanishad of the Atharvaveda carry that line and much more. They hold the famous image of two birds on one tree, the Jivatma and the Paramatma, one eating the fruit and one only watching. They lay out Para and Apara Vidya, the higher and lower knowledge, and promise that bhidyate hridaya granthih, the knots of the heart are cut, when the supreme is known. Thursday is the day kept for them, and Guru Purnima the festival. The reading takes about ten minutes. The page gives the full Devanagari text, an English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF for offline study.

Nine nights, nine forms, nine meditation verses, one for each day of Navaratri. Drawn from the Devi Bhagavatam and the Markandeya Purana, the Nava Durga Dhyanam gives the dhyanam, the visualisation, of all nine forms of Durga, which makes it essential for anyone who keeps the nine-night worship. Each verse draws down the particular blessing of its Navadurga form, and together they ask for protection from every evil and for spiritual strength. Tuesday and Friday are its days, with Navratri and Chaitra Navratri its festivals. The nine verses take about six minutes to read. On this page the complete text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration beside it, the Hindi meaning for each form, and a PDF you can download free for offline recitation.

At Tiruchanur, just below Tirumala, sits Padmavathi, the consort of Lord Venkateswara, and this hymn from the Tirumala tradition is sung to her. The Padmavathi Stotram, eight verses long, calls down her blessings, asks for marital bliss and harmony in relationships, and seeks the fulfilment of every cherished wish, all wrapped in the auspiciousness and grace she is known for. Friday is the day devotees keep for it, and Diwali its festival. The eight verses take around seven minutes to read with attention. The page sets out the complete text in Devanagari, with a clear English transliteration alongside, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a free PDF you can download and keep for chanting away from the screen.

Goswami Tulsidas wrote this for a wedding, the wedding of Shiva and Parvati, and devotees have been singing it at their own weddings ever since. The Parvati Mangal, ten verses in Tulsidas's hand, tells the whole story of that divine marriage. Reciting it brings marital harmony and bliss to married life; hearing or reading it helps the unmarried find an ideal partner; and it is sung through Hindu wedding ceremonies for auspiciousness and blessing. Monday is the day kept for it, and Maha Shivaratri, the night of Shiva and Parvati's union, its festival. The full reading runs to about fifteen minutes. On this page the complete text waits in Devanagari, with an English transliteration beside it, the Hindi meaning, and a free downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

The cosmic Person sacrificed so that the worlds might come to be, and from that sacrifice the seasons, the Vedas, and the four orders of society arose, this is what the Purusha Suktam sings. Found in the Rigveda at Mandala 10, Sukta 90, its eighteen verses praise Vishnu as that supreme being and carry knowledge of the cosmic creation itself. Recited at home or work, the hymn is held to purify the atmosphere and clear negativity, to draw prosperity and auspiciousness into a place, and to open the way to spiritual knowledge and self-realisation. Thursday and Sunday are its days, with Ekadashi its festival. The eighteen verses take about twelve minutes. The page gives the full Devanagari text, English transliteration, Hindi arth, and a free PDF.

Seven hundred dohas of Tulsidas, gathered around the name of Rama, that is the Ram Satsai. Drawn from Tulsidas's own Ram Satsai, this selection carries his couplets of practical spiritual wisdom, the kind meant to be carried into daily life rather than left on the page. Reading them strengthens devotion to Rama, cultivates compassion, humility, and right conduct, and works quietly to dissolve pride and ego through the wisdom each doha holds. Sunday is the day kept for it, and Ram Navami its festival. The fifteen verses here take about ten minutes to read. The page presents the complete selection in Devanagari, with an English transliteration set beside it, the Hindi meaning for each doha, and a PDF you can download at no cost for offline recitation.

Vishnu reclining on Adishesha at Srirangam is Ranganatha, and these eight verses, ascribed to Adi Shankaracharya within the Vaishnavite literature, are sung to that reclining form. The Ranganatha Ashtakam calls down the lord's blessing, is recited to relieve physical and mental ailments, the adhi-vyadhi, and points toward liberation and entry to Vishnuloka, all while purifying the mind and granting a settled spiritual bliss. Thursday and Sunday are its days, and Vaikuntha Ekadashi and Ekadashi its festivals. The eight verses take about eight minutes to read. On this page the full text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration alongside it, the Hindi arth verse by verse, and a free PDF for offline chanting.

Couples longing for a child have turned to this hymn for generations. Rooted in the traditional Santana Gopala upasana, the Santan Prapti Stotram is a focused prayer for the blessing of progeny, invoking the Santana Gopala form of Krishna, the child-protecting Krishna. Its ten verses are recited to guard a pregnancy and clear Garbha Dosha, and to bless childless couples with healthy offspring. Monday and Wednesday are the days kept for it, and Janmashtami its festival. The ten verses take about six minutes to read. The page lays out the complete text in Devanagari, with a clear English transliteration beside it, the Hindi meaning for each verse, and a free PDF you can download and keep for offline recitation.

Rahasya means secret, and this hymn opens the hidden, inner worship of Saraswati rather than the public one. Handed down through tradition, the Saraswati Rahasya Stotram carries powerful bija mantras for Saraswati sadhana within its seven verses. Reciting it is held to grant vak-siddhi, mastery over speech and eloquence, and to scatter the ignorance and intellectual darkness that dull the mind. Wednesday and Thursday are its days, and Vasant Panchami, the spring festival of the goddess of learning, its occasion. The seven verses take about eight minutes to read with attention. On this page the complete text is given in Devanagari, with an English transliteration set against it, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a PDF you can download free for offline practice.

Saubhagya is the good fortune that holds a marriage and a household together, and this hymn to Lakshmi is named for it. Coming down through tradition, the Saubhagya Lakshmi Stotram asks across its eight verses for marital bliss and auspiciousness, for relief from poverty and the arrival of financial stability, for progeny and family happiness, and for an all-round lift in luck and good fortune. Friday is the day devotees keep for it, and Diwali and Varalakshmi Vrata its festivals. The eight verses take about seven minutes to read. The page presents the complete text in Devanagari, with an English transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning line by line, and a free downloadable PDF so the hymn can be chanted away from the screen.

When Shani's gaze threatened his kingdom, King Dasharatha, the father of Rama, composed these verses to appease the slow planet, and that is how this stotra comes down to us in the Skanda Purana as the Dasharathakritam. Across twelve verses the Shani Stotra is recited to ease the afflictions of Saturn, the sade sati and the dhaiya, and for protection from accidents, chronic disease, and sudden losses, for Shani is the planet that grants a kingdom when pleased and strips it away when angered. Saturday is the day kept for it. The twelve verses take about eight minutes to read. The page gives the full text in Devanagari, an English transliteration beside it, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Before sleep, a few lines, and the night is handed over to the divine. The Shayan Prarthana, drawn from various scriptures, is a bedtime prayer of six verses gathered for exactly that hour. It invokes five powerful warrior deities to keep nightmares away, repeats the Achyuta-Ananta-Govinda names that guard a sleeper through the dark, and closes with the Shantakaram Vishnu shloka that settles the mind into peaceful, divine sleep. Folded in is the Kshama Prarthana, which asks forgiveness for every error of the day just ended. The six verses take about five minutes. On this page you can read the complete prayer in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration set beside it, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF to keep at the bedside.

Five syllables, Na-Ma-Shi-Va-Ya, and Adi Shankaracharya gave each one a verse. The Shiv Panchakshara Stotram unfolds the mystical meaning hidden in every syllable of Om Namah Shivaya across its five stanzas, and stays close enough to the mantra itself to serve as a mantra-based stotra for daily chanting. Reciting it deepens the devotional bond with Shiva, is said to open entry to Shivaloka after death, and bestows purity of mind and body. Monday is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the Shravan month its festivals. The five verses take about four minutes, short enough to learn by heart. Here the full text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration alongside it, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Five jewels offered at Shiva's feet. The Shiva Pancharatnam, handed down from the Purana tradition, is a short hymn of five verses meant for daily devotion and steady spiritual growth. It salutes Shiva in all his forms, asks for divine protection and auspiciousness, and is recited to clear away sins and bestow spiritual merit. Monday, Shiva's own day, is when it belongs, and Maha Shivaratri is its festival. At about three minutes the recitation is brief enough to begin or close a day without strain. On this page you can read all five verses in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration running beside them, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF to keep for offline chanting whenever you wish.

At Nathdwara stands Shrinathji, the form of Krishna who raised Govardhan hill on a fingertip, and this aarti is sung before that foremost shrine of the Pushti Marg. Belonging to the Nathdwara Pushti Marg tradition of Vallabhacharya, the Shrinathji Aarti describes the ashta-chaap, the eight-fold daily seva offered to the deity, across its five verses. It invokes Krishna as the child who lifted the hill to shelter his people, draws down his nurturing grace and an abundance both material and spiritual, and binds the devotee to Vallabhacharya's tradition. Wednesday and Sunday are its days, with Janmashtami, Annakut, and Govardhan Puja its festivals. The five verses take about five minutes. The page carries the full Devanagari text, English transliteration, Hindi arth, and a free PDF.

From the Ananda Ramayana comes this armour of Mata Sita, a kavacham of seven verses that wraps the devotee in her complete protection. The Sita Kavacham invokes Sita as Mahalakshmi, the source of all auspiciousness, asks for marital harmony and the strength of pativrata shakti, and bestows saubhagya, good fortune, especially on women who recite it. Friday is the day kept for it, and Sita Navami, Vivah Panchami, and Ram Navami are its festivals. The seven verses take about four minutes to read. On this page the complete text is set out in Devanagari, with an English transliteration alongside it, the Hindi meaning for each verse, and a free PDF you can download and keep for offline recitation whenever you turn to it.

Eight verses to the sun, the visible god whom anyone can see rise. This traditional Sanskrit Surya Ashtakam is recited to clear every planetary affliction and dosha from a chart, and is held to bless childless couples with progeny, to lift poverty and bring wealth, and to burn away even the great sins accumulated across lifetimes, with steady recitation said to lead to Surya Loka after death. Sunday, the sun's own day, is when it belongs, and Makar Sankranti, when the sun turns north, is its festival. The eight verses take about five minutes to read. The page presents the complete text in Devanagari, with a clear English transliteration beside it, the Hindi arth for each verse, and a free PDF for offline chanting.

This is the convocation address of ancient India, the Shiksha Valli of the Taittiriya Upanishad in the Krishna Yajurveda, twelve verses a teacher once spoke to departing students. It carries the immortal charge Satyam Vada, Dharmam Chara, speak the truth, walk in dharma, and the line that places mother, father, teacher, and guest as gods: matri-devo, pitri-devo, acharya-devo, atithi-devo bhava. It opens with the Saha Navavatu mantra for harmony between guru and student and grants medha, brahma-varchas, and fame, the foundation of Indian ethical and spiritual education. Thursday is its day, and Guru Purnima its festival. The reading takes about fifteen minutes. The page gives the full Devanagari text, English transliteration, Hindi arth, and a free PDF.

Shiva and Parvati are worshipped here not apart but as one divine couple, Uma and Maheshwara together. This traditional Sanskrit hymn of eleven verses, the Uma Maheshwara Stotram, asks for marital harmony and the strengthening of conjugal bonds, draws down the combined blessings of Shiva and Shakti, and is recited to clear sins and grant Shivaloka after death, with auspiciousness, beauty, and prosperity along the way, even said to dispel the negativity of Kali Yuga. Monday and Friday are its days, and Maha Shivaratri, the Shravan month, and Gauri Tritiya its festivals. The eleven verses take about seven minutes. On this page the complete text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration beside it, the Hindi meaning, and a free downloadable PDF for offline recitation.

When a house is built against the rules of direction, tradition says the dwelling itself falls ill, and this mantra is the remedy. Built on the Rigvedic Vastu Suktam and traditional verses, the Vastu Dosh Nivaran Mantra runs to nine verses recited to clear Vastu defects from home and workplace, to invoke the protection of the Dikpalas, the deities of the directions, and to neutralise the negative energies that wrong construction sets loose, leaving peace, prosperity, and positive energy in the house. Thursday and Sunday are its days, and Griha Pravesh, the house-warming, its occasion. The nine verses take about six minutes. The page presents the full text in Devanagari, with English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF for offline use.

Eight verses of pure praise to Vishnu, the preserver of the worlds, drawn from the traditional Vaishnavite literature. The Vishnu Ashtakam is recited to see desires fulfilled when chanted with devotion, to lift sins and purify the soul, and to draw protection from every adversity, leaving peace, prosperity, and happiness in their place. Thursday and Sunday, Vishnu's own days, are when it belongs, and Ekadashi is its festival. The eight verses take about eight minutes to read with care. On this page you can read the complete Vishnu Ashtakam in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration set beside it, study the Hindi meaning for each verse, and download a free PDF to keep for offline recitation whenever the day or the festival calls for it.

A chalisa takes its name from the forty chaupais at its core, with framing dohas bringing the full recitation to forty-three verse-units. The Vitthal Chalisa, born of the Varkari bhakti tradition of Maharashtra, sings forty verses in praise of Vitthal, the Panduranga of Pandharpur, and celebrates the great Varkari saint lineage that gathers at his feet. Recited with devotion, it is said to fulfil heartfelt wishes and to clear poverty and disease while granting progeny and wisdom. Thursday and Saturday are its days, and Ashadhi Ekadashi and Kartiki Ekadashi, the two great pilgrimage days of Pandharpur, its festivals. The reading takes about seven minutes. The page gives the full Devanagari text, an English transliteration beside it, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF for offline chanting.
